title: dec-prob-01
scenario: Your hiring loop has three independent stages — screen, technical, panel — and a candidate you like has an 80% chance of passing each one.
prompt: What is the probability she passes all three stages?

choices.text: 51.2% — the three 80% chances multiply: 0.8 × 0.8 × 0.8.
choices.rationale: Independent probabilities multiply: 0.8³ = 0.512. Each added stage compounds the chance of a miss.

choices.text: 80% — each stage is 80%, so the loop is 80%.
choices.rationale: That's the averaging reflex. The loop only succeeds if every stage does, and three 80% hurdles stack to 51.2%.

choices.text: 40% — start at 100% and subtract 20% for each stage.
choices.rationale: Failure chances don't add. Subtracting 20% three times overcounts the ways to fail; multiplying gives 51.2%.

choices.text: 48.8% — the chance the stages go her way.
choices.rationale: 48.8% is the complement — the chance at least one stage fails (1 − 0.512). Passing all three is 51.2%.

explanation: Independent chances multiply, they don't add or average: 0.8³ = 51.2%. A chain of individually likely events is much less likely than any single link — which is why strong candidates die in long loops.

title: dec-prob-02
scenario: Your checkout flow calls three independent services — payments, inventory, fraud — and each one meets its latency SLA on 90% of requests.
prompt: What fraction of checkouts have at least one service blow its SLA?

choices.text: 27.1% — one minus the chance all three are fast: 1 − 0.9³ = 1 − 0.729.
choices.rationale: 'At least one' is the complement trick: compute P(none fail) = 0.9³ = 0.729, then subtract from 1.

choices.text: 10% — the flow is only as slow as its weakest link.
choices.rationale: Each service alone misses 10% of the time, but the checkout is exposed to all three misses, not just one: 27.1%.

choices.text: 30% — add the three 10% miss rates.
choices.rationale: Adding double-counts requests where two or three services miss together. The exact answer is 1 − 0.729 = 27.1%.

choices.text: 72.9% — multiply the reliabilities.
choices.rationale: 0.9³ = 72.9% is the chance everything is fast — the complement of what was asked.

explanation: For 'at least one failure', don't add failure rates — take the complement of everything succeeding: 1 − 0.9³ = 27.1%. Chained dependencies quietly turn three reliable services into a flow that stumbles on 1 in 4 requests.

title: dec-prob-03
scenario: Out of every 1,000 shipments your marketplace handles, 10 involve fraud (1%). Your detector flags 90% of fraudulent shipments — but also flags 10% of legitimate ones. A shipment just got flagged, and ops wants to freeze the seller's account on the spot.
prompt: Given only the flag, what's the probability this shipment is actually fraudulent?

choices.text: About 8.3% — of every 1,000 shipments, 9 flags are true fraud and 99 are false alarms, so a flag is fraud 9 times in 108.
choices.rationale: Run the counts: 10 fraudulent → 9 flagged; 990 legitimate → 99 flagged. P(fraud | flag) = 9 / (9 + 99) = 9/108 = 1/12.

choices.text: 90% — the detector catches 90% of fraud, so a flag is 90% reliable.
choices.rationale: That inverts the conditional. 90% is P(flag | fraud); the question asks P(fraud | flag), and the 990 legitimate shipments feed the flag pool 99 false alarms.

choices.text: 80% — 90% detection minus the 10% false-alarm rate.
choices.rationale: Subtracting the two rates has no probabilistic meaning. The base rate is what matters: false alarms outnumber true hits 99 to 9.

choices.text: 1% — the flag doesn't change the underlying fraud rate.
choices.rationale: The flag is real evidence — it moves the probability from 1% up to 8.3%. It just doesn't move it anywhere near 90%.

explanation: When the base rate is low, even a good test's alarms are mostly false: 9 true flags versus 99 false ones makes a flag only 1-in-12 fraud. Never read the test's accuracy as the alarm's accuracy — condition on the evidence, not the reverse.

title: dec-ev-01
scenario: A product manager pitches a feature that costs $8,000 to build. She estimates a 20% chance it unlocks $50,000 in new revenue, and an 80% chance it moves nothing.
prompt: What is the expected value of building it?

choices.text: +$2,000 — a 20% shot at $50,000 is worth $10,000, minus the $8,000 build cost.
choices.rationale: EV is the probability-weighted payoff net of cost: 0.2 × $50,000 − $8,000 = +$2,000.

choices.text: +$42,000 — $50,000 of revenue minus the $8,000 cost.
choices.rationale: That's the best case, not the expectation. The $50,000 only arrives 20% of the time, so it's worth $10,000 up front.
That's the expected revenue, but the $8,000 build cost is paid in every branch. Net EV is +$2,000.

choices.text: Negative — it fails four times out of five, so it's a losing bet.
choices.rationale: Frequency of failure isn't EV. A bet that usually loses can still be worth taking when the win is big enough: $10,000 expected against $8,000 spent.

explanation: A decision's worth is the probability-weighted sum of its outcomes minus what you pay to play: 0.2 × 50,000 − 8,000 = +$2,000. Neither the best case nor the most likely case is the value of the bet.

title: dec-ev-02
scenario: You're suing a former partner. They've offered a $60,000 settlement. Going to trial costs $20,000 in fees win or lose, and a win pays $200,000 while a loss pays nothing.
prompt: Above what win probability does trial beat the settlement?

choices.text: 40% — trial EV is p × $200,000 − $20,000, which passes $60,000 when p exceeds 40%.
choices.rationale: Set the two options equal: p × 200,000 − 20,000 = 60,000 gives p = 80,000 / 200,000 = 40%. That break-even is the price of the bet.

choices.text: 30% — the settlement divided by the award: 60,000 / 200,000.
choices.rationale: That ignores the $20,000 in fees you pay either way at trial. The fees raise the bar to (60,000 + 20,000) / 200,000 = 40%.

choices.text: 20% — the settlement net of fees, divided by the award.
choices.rationale: The fees are a cost of choosing trial, not a discount on the settlement — they make trial need a higher win rate, not a lower one.

choices.text: 50% — go to trial only if you're more likely to win than lose.
choices.rationale: The coin-flip threshold ignores the payoffs. A $200,000 award against a $60,000 offer makes trial correct well below 50% — from 40% up.

explanation: The break-even probability — where the EVs of the two options cross — is the real price of a gamble. Here p × 200,000 − 20,000 = 60,000 solves to 40%: your lawyer's job is to tell you which side of that line your case sits on.

title: dec-ev-03
scenario: Last quarter, with $10M in the bank, you chose launch plan A — a 60% chance of gaining $700,000, a 40% chance of losing $300,000 — over plan B, a guaranteed $150,000. Plan A missed: you lost $300,000. The board asks whether the decision was a mistake.

choices.text: No — A's EV was 0.6 × $700k − 0.4 × $300k = +$300,000, double B's sure $150,000, and the company could easily absorb the loss. The outcome doesn't rewrite the decision.
choices.rationale: Judge the decision by the information and odds at the time. A 40%-likely loss landing is exactly what a good 60/40 bet does four times in ten.

choices.text: Yes — it lost $300,000, and results are what count.
choices.rationale: That's results-oriented hindsight. Grading choices by single outcomes teaches you to refuse every good bet that happens to lose.

choices.text: Yes — a guaranteed $150,000 should always be taken over a gamble.
choices.rationale: Certainty has some value, but not $150,000 of EV — and with $10M in the bank, a $300,000 downside is nowhere near the size where risk aversion should override expectation.

choices.text: The loss itself proves the 60% estimate was inflated.
choices.rationale: One miss is almost no evidence against a 60% estimate — genuine 60/40 bets miss 40% of the time. It would take a run of outcomes to indict the number.

explanation: Decision quality lives in the process — the odds, payoffs, and stakes as known at the time — not in the single outcome that happened to land. A +$300,000-EV bet the balance sheet can absorb was right before the coin flipped, and it's still right after.

title: dec-margin-01
scenario: You've spent $10,000 on ad channel A and it has returned $30,000 — a 3x average return. But the next $1,000 in A would bring back about $1,500, while the next $1,000 in untapped channel B would bring back about $2,500.

choices.text: Channel B — the next dollar earns $2.50 there versus $1.50 in A, and the next dollar is the only one you're deciding about.
choices.rationale: Decisions are made at the margin. A's 3x history describes money already spent, not the dollar in your hand.

choices.text: Channel A — it's returning 3x, the best number on the board.
choices.rationale: 3x is the average over past spend. Averages flatter saturated channels; the marginal return in A has already fallen to 1.5x.

choices.text: Split it $500 and $500 to stay diversified.
choices.rationale: Splitting earns roughly $750 + $1,250 = $2,000 — less than the $2,500 from putting the whole increment where its marginal return is highest.

choices.text: Neither — A's declining returns mean the ad budget is exhausted.
choices.rationale: Declining isn't negative: A's marginal $1,500 on $1,000 still profits, and B's $2,500 certainly does. The question is ranking margins, not quitting.

explanation: Allocate by marginal return, not average return: the only question the next dollar asks is where it earns the most, and past performance answers a different question. Here that's $2.50 in B against $1.50 in A.

title: dec-margin-02
scenario: Your startup has sunk $400,000 into feature X. Finishing it needs $100,000 more and would generate $150,000 in value. Redirecting that same $100,000 to a pivot would generate $220,000.
prompt: What should you do with the $100,000?

choices.text: Pivot — from here, $100,000 buys $220,000 one way and $150,000 the other. The $400,000 is spent in both futures.
choices.rationale: Only the forward-looking margin differs between your options: +$120,000 net versus +$50,000. Sunk spend cancels out of the comparison.

choices.text: Finish X — abandoning it now would waste the $400,000 already invested.
choices.rationale: That's sunk-cost anchoring. The $400,000 is unrecoverable under every option; honoring it costs you the $70,000 the pivot wins by.

choices.text: Finish X — $150,000 back on $100,000 is a healthy 50% return.
choices.rationale: 'Is it good?' is the wrong question — the question is 'is it better than the next-best use?' The pivot returns 120% on the same money.

choices.text: Do neither and hold the $100,000 in reserve.
choices.rationale: Holding returns $0 against forward gains of $50,000 or $120,000. Caution here is the worst-EV branch on the tree.

explanation: Compare futures, not histories: every option shares the $400,000 already spent, so the decision reduces to $220,000 versus $150,000 for the same marginal $100,000. What you've invested is a fact about the past, not a reason.

title: dec-margin-03
scenario: Capacity forces your agency to drop one of two clients. The flagship account bills $500,000 a year, consumes $440,000 in direct delivery cost, and requires two dedicated support engineers costing $80,000 who exist only for that account. The small account bills $120,000 against $70,000 of cost. The room wants to keep the flagship.
prompt: Purely on the numbers, which client should you keep?

choices.text: The small one — it clears +$50,000, while the flagship's true marginal profit is $500k − $440k − $80k = −$20,000. The felt winner loses money.
choices.rationale: Every cost that disappears when the client does is marginal — including the dedicated engineers. Counted fully, the flagship's sign flips negative.

choices.text: The flagship — $500,000 in revenue dwarfs $120,000.
choices.rationale: Top-line anchoring. Revenue isn't profit: the flagship spends $520,000 to collect $500,000, so its size measures the loss, not the win.

choices.text: The flagship — before the support team it earns $500k − $440k = +$60,000, and dedicated staff belong in overhead.
choices.rationale: Those engineers were hired for this client and leave with it — that makes their $80,000 a marginal cost of keeping it, not shared overhead. Buried allocation is how losing accounts hide.

choices.text: The flagship — a $500,000 logo is worth keeping for credibility alone.
choices.rationale: The prompt asks about the numbers, and the numbers say you'd pay $20,000 a year for the logo while turning away $50,000. If prestige is worth $70,000+, price it explicitly — don't smuggle it in.

explanation: The margin includes every cost that exists only because the option does. Move the dedicated team from 'overhead' to where it belongs and the flagship's profit flips from +$60k to −$20k — the delta doesn't just shrink, it changes sign against the intuitive winner.

title: dec-gto-01
scenario: Heads-up at the river, the pot is $100 and your opponent bets $50. You hold a bluff-catcher: you win if and only if he's bluffing.
prompt: What fraction of the time must he be bluffing for your call to break even?

choices.text: 25% — you risk $50 to win the $150 out there, so you need 50 / (100 + 50 + 50) of the final pot.
choices.rationale: Pot-odds break-even is bet / (pot + bet + your call): 50/200 = 25%. Win a quarter of the time and the call is free.

choices.text: 33.3% — your $50 against the $150 in the middle.
choices.rationale: That forgets your own call goes into the pot you're winning back. The final pot is $200, so the bar is 50/200 = 25%.

choices.text: 50% — a call has to be right more often than wrong.
choices.rationale: The even-money reflex ignores the price. You're paid 3-to-1, so being right just over one time in four is enough.

choices.text: 75% — a river bet usually means it, so you need to be nearly sure.
choices.rationale: How sure you need to be is set by the pot, not by vibes: the $150 already out there subsidizes your call down to 25%.

explanation: The pot sets the price of a call: break-even equity = bet / (pot + bet + call) = 50/200 = 25%. Before asking whether he's bluffing, know the number the bluffing frequency has to beat.

title: dec-gto-02
scenario: A rival firm keeps threatening lawsuits over a disputed $100,000 escrow your company holds. Mounting a suit costs them $50,000 in non-refundable legal fees, and their standard settlement demand is the escrow plus those fees — so caving hands them a net $100,000, while fighting a hollow claim to judgment costs them their $50,000. Some of their claims are genuine; you can't tell which from the filing.
prompt: At minimum, how often must you fight these claims so that a hollow one loses them money?

choices.text: Two-thirds of the time — a hollow claim earns f × $100k − (1 − f) × $50k if you cave with frequency f, which hits zero at f = 1/3.
choices.rationale: This is the minimum-defense-frequency structure: defend at least pot/(pot + bet) = 100/150 = 2/3, capping their bluff EV at zero.

choices.text: One-third of the time — match their one-third cost-to-prize ratio.
choices.rationale: One-third is the most you may cave, not the least you must fight. Fighting only 1/3 lets a hollow claim earn 2/3 × 100k − 1/3 × 50k = +$50,000.

choices.text: Half the time — split the difference so you're unpredictable.
choices.rationale: 50/50 sounds balanced but still pays them: 0.5 × 100k − 0.5 × 50k = +$25,000 per hollow claim. The threshold comes from their payoffs, not from symmetry.

choices.text: Every time — never reward a threat.
choices.rationale: Always fighting does kill hollow claims, but it isn't the minimum — and against their genuine claims it maximizes your own legal spend. Equilibrium prices deterrence, it doesn't buy it at any cost.

explanation: To make empty aggression unprofitable, defend at least pot/(pot + bet) — here 100/(100 + 50) = 2/3. Fold more than a third and every bluff they fire is +EV, so they should fire them all; deterrence is a frequency, not a mood.

title: dec-gto-03
scenario: At a negotiation workshop you play a bluffing game: $100 of chips sit in the pot, and on your turn you may push $100 while claiming the winning card — sometimes you have it, sometimes you don't. Your counterpart can call your $100 or fold. You want your pushes balanced so calling can't beat you.
prompt: What share of your pushes should be bluffs to make her calls exactly indifferent?

choices.text: One-third — she calls $100 to win $300, so she needs to win 1 time in 3, and bluffing exactly 1/3 of your pushes gives her exactly that.
choices.rationale: Balanced bluffing share is bet/(pot + 2 × bet) = 100/300 = 1/3: two value pushes for every bluff, making her call EV precisely zero.

choices.text: Half — balanced means half real, half bluff.
choices.rationale: At 50% bluffs her call wins half the time while needing only a third: EV = 0.5 × 200 − 0.5 × 100 = +$50 per call. 'Balanced' means priced to the pot, not split down the middle.

choices.text: Two-thirds — bluff twice for every real push to apply maximum pressure.
choices.rationale: That inverts the ratio. Winning 2/3 of calls when she needs 1/3, she snap-calls everything and prints money: 2/3 × 200 − 1/3 × 100 = +$100 per call.

choices.text: Zero — only push with the winning card, so pushing can never lose.
choices.rationale: The never-bluff strategy is transparent: she correctly folds every time, your value pushes win only the pot and never her $100, and you've left the whole extra street on the table.

explanation: Price your bluffs off the caller's odds: she risks B to win P + 2B, so bluff exactly B/(P + 2B) — here 100/300 = 1/3 — and her call and fold become equally worthless. Equilibrium isn't maximizing any one push; it's making the other side's options not matter.

title: dec-bank-01
scenario: Your startup has $50,000 of runway. A supplier offers a deal that, for whatever you put in, has a 60% chance of doubling it and a 40% chance of losing it all — genuinely +EV at 20 cents per dollar committed.
prompt: How much should you put in?

choices.text: A minority slice — say $10,000. The edge pays at any size, but only a survivable size lets you keep playing.
choices.rationale: EV per dollar is +$0.20 whether you bet $10k or $50k; what changes with size is the 40% branch, which at full stake ends the company.

choices.text: All $50,000 — the bet is +EV, so more money in means more EV out.
choices.rationale: All-in-because-+EV ignores ruin: four times in ten you're dead and can never take another edge. Expectation math assumes you survive to keep collecting it.

choices.text: Nothing — a 40% chance of losing the money is too dangerous.
choices.rationale: Risk alone isn't a veto. At $10,000 the worst case stings but doesn't kill, and refusing forgoes free expected value — knowing the bet is good is half the skill; sizing it is the other half.

choices.text: $30,000 — commit 60% of the runway to match the 60% win probability.
choices.rationale: 'Bet your win probability' is a reflex, not a rule — win chance says nothing about how much downside you can absorb. Lose this one (40% of the time!) and $20,000 of runway remains.

explanation: Whether a bet is +EV and how much to put on it are separate questions. Per-dollar edge is constant across sizes; ruin is not — the right stake is one whose losing branch leaves you in the game.

title: dec-bank-02
scenario: Your sports model genuinely wins 60% of its picks at even money, and you're staking a $10,000 bankroll one bet at a time, reinvesting as you go.
prompt: What fraction of the bankroll per bet maximizes long-run growth?

choices.text: 20% — for even money the growth-optimal (Kelly) fraction is win probability minus loss probability: 0.6 − 0.4.
choices.rationale: Kelly for even-money bets is p − q = 20%. Below it you grow slower than you could; above it extra variance eats the extra edge.

choices.text: 60% — stake your win probability.
choices.rationale: Betting your win probability triples the Kelly fraction. A perfectly ordinary 3-loss run at 60% stakes turns $10,000 into $640 — the edge never gets a chance to work.

choices.text: 100% — the edge is real, so compound it as hard as possible.
choices.rationale: At full stakes the first loss — 40% per bet — zeroes the bankroll, and ruin is permanent. Long-run growth at 100% staking is guaranteed zero.

choices.text: 40% — double the aggressive-looking 20% to press the advantage harder.
choices.rationale: Growth peaks at Kelly and falls past it: at double-Kelly, long-run growth collapses back to roughly zero while the swings double. More stake stops meaning more growth.

explanation: Growth is maximized at the Kelly fraction — for even money, p − q = 20% here — and it's a peak, not a floor: over-betting a real edge doesn't accelerate compounding, it destroys it. How much is a different question from whether.

title: dec-bank-03
scenario: Your firm holds $1M in cash against a $300,000 monthly payroll. A client offers a fixed-fee contract: 80% chance it nets you $500,000, but a 20% chance of a penalty scenario that costs you $900,000. The EV is 0.8 × $500k − 0.2 × $900k = +$220,000, and your CFO wants to sign as-is.
prompt: What's the right call on the contract as offered?

choices.text: Don't take it at this size — insure the penalty, syndicate it, or renegotiate. It's +$220,000 in EV, and it still risks missing payroll one time in five.
choices.rationale: The losing branch leaves $100,000 against a $300,000 payroll — functional ruin. A bet can be clearly +EV and still be too big for the bankroll behind it.

choices.text: Sign it — +$220,000 of expected value is +$220,000; refusing positive EV is leaving money on the table.
choices.rationale: EV averages over futures, including the one where the company dies before the average arrives. All-in-because-+EV is exactly how winners go broke.

choices.text: Sign it — it pays off 80% of the time, so you're overwhelmingly likely to be fine.
choices.rationale: Best-case reading. One-in-five is not remote — it's a die roll — and this die roll ends the firm. Likelihood of the good branch says nothing about survivability of the bad one.

choices.text: Decline outright — a $900,000 downside against a $500,000 upside makes it a losing bet on its face.
choices.rationale: Right instinct, wrong math: weighted by probability it's +$220,000, comfortably a winning bet. The problem is its size relative to your cash, which is why shrinking or laying off risk beats walking away.

explanation: +EV is necessary, not sufficient: when the losing branch is ruin, no expectation justifies full exposure. The professional move isn't take-it-or-leave-it — it's resizing the bet (insurance, syndication, renegotiation) until the downside is survivable and the edge is still yours.

title: dec-exploit-01
scenario: Scouting an opposing keeper before a penalty shootout, your analyst finds he has dived to his left on all eight recorded penalties this season.

choices.text: His right, every kick, until he shows he's adjusted.
choices.rationale: Against an opponent with a fixed pattern, the maximally exploitative line is a pure strategy at his weakness. Mixing is protection against adaptation — spend it only when he adapts.

choices.text: Keep mixing sides 50/50 — staying unpredictable always comes first.
choices.rationale: Unpredictability defends against an opponent who reads you. This one isn't reading anyone — mixing sends half your kicks into his dive for no defensive gain.

choices.text: His left — he'll expect you to have scouted him and switch.
choices.rationale: That prices in a level of thinking he's shown no sign of. Eight-for-eight is evidence of a habit, not a trap; play the data, not the mind game you imagined.

choices.text: Down the middle as a compromise between the two reads.
choices.rationale: There's nothing to compromise between — the read points one way. Averaging a strong signal with its opposite just dilutes the edge.

explanation: When the opponent's strategy is fixed and known, the best response is pure: hammer the weakness until it moves. Balance is what you buy when they can punish you — against a pattern, it's paying for insurance you don't need.

title: dec-exploit-02
scenario: Over a long online session you've confirmed a regular folds to river bets 80% of the time. Pots are running about $100 when you bet $50 — so a stone-cold bluff earns 0.8 × $100 − 0.2 × $50 = +$70. At equilibrium he'd need to fold no more than a third of the time.
prompt: How should you play rivers against him?

choices.text: Bluff far more often than equilibrium — and track his calls, because a leak this profitable gets noticed once you start hammering it.
choices.rationale: An 80% fold rate against a 33.3% cap makes over-bluffing hugely +EV, but the deviation is visible. The exploit lasts exactly as long as the read does.

choices.text: Stay balanced — deviating from GTO is how you get exploited.
choices.rationale: Equilibrium is the fallback when you have no read, not the ceiling when you do. Refusing a confirmed +$70-per-bluff edge is its own leak.

choices.text: Bluff every river from here on — the number says it prints money, so lock it in.
choices.rationale: The number is a snapshot. Every deviation opens a door back at you: once he starts calling, each unmonitored bluff burns $50, and 'always' has no tripwire for noticing.

choices.text: Stop bluffing him — someone folding that much only continues with hands that beat you.
choices.rationale: Backwards: heavy folding is what makes bluffs profitable, not dangerous. His 20% continues do beat your air — which is priced in at +$70 a bet.

explanation: Exploitation is a bet on your read: deviate hard while the leak holds, and instrument for the moment it closes. Max-exploit without monitoring the counter-adjustment isn't aggression — it's leaving the door open with your money behind it.

title: dec-exploit-03
scenario: Your insurer faces a category of $150,000 injury claims. Fighting one in court costs $50,000 in legal fees: against a fraudulent claim you win and pay nothing, against a genuine one you pay the $150,000 anyway, plus the fees. Audits show only 10% of claims in this category are fraudulent. The claims chief's policy is to fight, on principle: 'settling teaches fraudsters we're soft.'
prompt: On the numbers, what's the right policy for this category?

choices.text: Settle them — fighting only breaks even when at least a third are fraudulent, and at 10% each fight costs 0.1 × $100k saved − 0.9 × $50k burned = −$35,000 on average.
choices.rationale: Versus settling, fighting saves $100,000 against fraud but wastes $50,000 against genuine claims: break-even fraud rate is 50/150 = 1/3, and 10% is nowhere close.

choices.text: Fight every claim — never reward fraud, whatever each case costs.
choices.rationale: Principle at −$35,000 per claim is a $35,000 donation, 90% of it spent fighting honest customers. Deterrence has a price, and this one is above its value.

choices.text: Fight them — winning saves $100,000 and only costs $50,000, so fighting pays 2-to-1.
choices.rationale: That's the best case read as the expected case. The 2-to-1 payoff lands just 10% of the time; the other 90% you pay the claim and the fees.

choices.text: Fight half of them at random so claimants can't predict you.
choices.rationale: Unpredictability isn't free — each fight still runs −$35,000 on average, so mixing buys deterrence at half the full-fight loss. If the read is 10%, the EV-max response is pure.

explanation: The maximally exploitative response to a known population is pure: settle, because 10% fraud is far below the 1/3 break-even. But every deviation opens a door — a publicized never-fight policy invites the fraud rate to climb toward the threshold, so the read that justified the exploit must be re-audited, and the fights resumed the moment it stops being true.
